 {"id":"franken_networkx-8qan","title":"parity: dag_longest_path_length missing weight/default_weight params","description":"dag_longest_path_length(g) is missing params: weight (edge attr), default_weight (fallback). FNX signature: (g). NX signature: (G, weight='weight', default_weight=1). Add these for weighted DAG path length support.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T12:34:44.930205324Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-21T12:38:07.056443987Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T12:38:07.054366694Z","close_reason":"Added wrapper delegating to NetworkX for weight/default_weight params","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"franken_networkx-8rix","title":"Harden graph edit local parity conformance","description":"Add focused differential/no-fallback tests for the local graph edit path implementation, covering callback costs, upper_bound behavior, directed mismatch, and unsupported modes now that generated public coverage reports zero direct NetworkX delegations.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-18T06:08:32.752666620Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-18T06:10:35.756281342Z","closed_at":"2026-04-18T06:10:35.754010415Z","close_reason":"Added graph-edit no-fallback conformance coverage for callback costs, upper_bound, directed mismatch, and unsupported modes","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"franken_networkx-8sga","title":"parity: conversion live views simple EdgeView should satisfy mapping protocol","description":"The top-level to_directed(Graph) and to_undirected(DiGraph) live views currently fail NetworkX's simple EdgeView mapping contract. In FrankenNetworkX, dict(view.edges) silently produces {'a': 'b'}-style payloads and view.edges.get((u, v), default) is missing, while upstream NetworkX returns {(u, v): data} mappings and supports tuple-key get(). Match the upstream simple EdgeView mapping contract on conversion live views.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T10:52:30.923200121Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-22T11:16:50.048177455Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T11:16:32.821247668Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"comments":[{"id":7,"issue_id":"franken_networkx-8sga","author":"ubuntu","text":"Fixed in 11ba7d2. Added __getitem__/__contains__ on _ConversionEdgeView (routed through view.adj which already carries the directionality semantics) + bound _adjacency_view_get/keys/items/values via in-class assignment (class is defined after the helpers, so inline binding avoids forward-ref ordering issues). dict(view.edges) now produces {(u,v): attrs}, get((u,v), default) works, and __contains__ is tuple-aware. New test_conversion_live_view_edges_satisfies_mapping_protocol parametrized over to_directed(Graph) and to_undirected(DiGraph).","created_at":"2026-04-22T11:16:50Z"}]}
-{"id":"franken_networkx-8ud1m","title":"edge_connectivity returns weighted maxflow instead of edge cardinality on weighted graphs","description":"Differential scan against upstream nx turned up a silent-incorrect bug.

``fnx.edge_connectivity(G, s, t)`` returns the **capacity-weighted max-flow value**
when G has a ``capacity`` edge attribute, rather than the **edge cardinality**
of the minimum s-t edge cut (which is the documented ``edge_connectivity``
definition and what upstream nx returns).

Reproducer::

    import franken_networkx as fnx, networkx as nx

    # Graph with capacity attrs
    g = fnx.DiGraph()
    g.add_edges_from([
        ('s','a',{'capacity':10}),
        ('s','b',{'capacity':5}),
        ('a','t',{'capacity':10}),
        ('b','t',{'capacity':10}),
        ('a','b',{'capacity':2}),
    ])
    gn = nx.DiGraph()
    gn.add_edges_from([
        ('s','a',{'capacity':10}),
        ('s','b',{'capacity':5}),
        ('a','t',{'capacity':10}),
        ('b','t',{'capacity':10}),
        ('a','b',{'capacity':2}),
    ])

    # nx returns 2 — there are 2 edges leaving s, min cut is {s-a, s-b}
    # fnx returns 15.0 — maxflow with those capacities
    assert nx.edge_connectivity(gn, 's', 't') == 2
    assert fnx.edge_connectivity(g,  's', 't') == 2   # FAILS — returns 15.0

On unweighted graphs the bug is invisible because cardinality ==
maxflow when all edges have implicit capacity 1. It only manifests
when the caller has already put ``capacity`` attrs on edges for
flow algorithms.

Why this matters:

- ``edge_connectivity`` is a structural graph property — unit of
  measurement is "number of edges", not "flow units". A graph that
  needs 2 edges cut is 2-edge-connected regardless of how heavy
  those edges are.
- Anyone computing k-edge-connectedness via ``edge_connectivity(G) >= k``
  on a weighted graph silently gets wrong answers.
- Downstream: ``is_k_edge_connected`` and related predicates may
  also inherit this error depending on their internal dispatch.

Likely root cause:

- fnx's ``edge_connectivity`` calls the max-flow primitive and
  returns the flow value instead of reducing the graph to unit
  capacities (1 per edge) first.

Fix plan:

1. In fnx.edge_connectivity: strip or override the ``capacity``
   attr so every edge has capacity 1 before running the underlying
   max-flow, then return the integer cardinality.
2. Audit ``is_k_edge_connected``, ``all_node_cuts``,
   ``minimum_edge_cut`` for the same issue — minimum_edge_cut on
   the same input returns a valid cut (different tie-break from
   nx but same size), so it's probably fine, but double-check.
3. Regression test: one-line differential vs nx on a weighted
   capacity-attr graph.

Surfaced by /testing-conformance-harnesses scan of flow/cut family.
Same scan also flagged ``minimum_edge_cut`` returning a different
(but equivalent-size) cut set — that's a tie-break divergence, not
a correctness bug, so not filed.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-23T17:44:19.486272472Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-23T17:44:19.486272472Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

Same scan also flagged ``minimum_edge_cut`` returning a different

 {"id":"franken_networkx-y7qf","title":"parity: graph classes should expose dict factory attributes and multigraph key factories","description":"Graph-family classes currently lack NetworkX's dict-factory attributes adjlist_inner_dict_factory, adjlist_outer_dict_factory, edge_attr_dict_factory, graph_attr_dict_factory, node_attr_dict_factory, and node_dict_factory. MultiGraph and MultiDiGraph also lack edge_key_dict_factory and new_edge_key(). Upstream exposes all of these; the dict factories materialize empty dicts by default, and new_edge_key('a','b') returns 1 on a graph that already has key 'k1'. Match the upstream factory/helper surface across the core graph classes.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T07:45:20.207108301Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-21T22:58:49.728664011Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T22:58:49.726847624Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
-{"id":"franken_networkx-y87ra","title":"immediate_dominators includes start node; upstream nx excludes it","description":"Minor parity divergence turned up by the /testing-conformance-harnesses
scan on DAG algorithms.

``fnx.immediate_dominators(G, start)`` includes a ``{start: start}``
entry in the returned dict. Upstream ``nx.immediate_dominators``
excludes the start node per its documented contract (see nx
docstring quoted below).

Reproducer::

    import franken_networkx as fnx, networkx as nx

    gn = nx.DiGraph(); gn.add_edges_from([(0,1),(0,2),(1,3),(2,3),(3,4),(3,5)])
    gf = fnx.DiGraph(); gf.add_edges_from([(0,1),(0,2),(1,3),(2,3),(3,4),(3,5)])

    # nx  -> {1: 0, 2: 0, 3: 0, 4: 3, 5: 3}        (no key 0)
    # fnx -> {0: 0, 1: 0, 2: 0, 3: 0, 4: 3, 5: 3}  (includes 0 -> 0)
    assert fnx.immediate_dominators(gf, 0) == nx.immediate_dominators(gn, 0)  # FAILS

Upstream nx's docstring (verbatim)::

    idom : dict keyed by nodes
        A dict containing the immediate dominators of each node reachable from
        `start`, **except for `start` itself**.

Impact:

- Parity tests that do ``dict(fnx.immediate_dominators(G, s)) ==
  dict(nx.immediate_dominators(G, s))`` fail on the start-key
  presence.
- Users iterating ``.items()`` get an unexpected ``(start, start)``
  pair that their nx code wouldn't have produced. Practical effect
  depends on downstream code; most uses are robust to the extra
  entry because ``idom[start] == start`` is a self-loop and
  dominator-tree construction typically filters self-dominators.

Note: the existing fnx test at
``tests/python/test_dag_additional.py::TestImmediateDominators::test_chain``
explicitly asserts ``idom["a"] == "a"`` (the start-included shape),
so that test encodes the divergence and would need an update in
lockstep with the fix.

Fix plan:

1. ``python/franken_networkx/__init__.py::immediate_dominators`` (or
   wherever it dispatches from _fnx) — drop the ``{start: start}``
   entry from the returned dict before returning.
2. Update ``test_dag_additional.py::test_chain``, ``test_diamond``,
   etc. to no longer assert on ``idom[root]``.
3. Regression test: differential against ``nx.immediate_dominators``
   on a small DAG + start pair.

Priority: P3 — semantic divergence but inclusion is a superset of
the nx output (no dominator value is wrong, just an extra self-loop
entry).

Surfaced by /testing-conformance-harnesses scan; one of ~35 probe
calls across the DAG family, no other DAG divergences (the
topological_sort / dag_longest_path mismatches are legitimate
tie-break divergences — both results are valid — not filed).","status":"in_progress","priority":3,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-23T17:45:25.512740513Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-23T17:45:52.535058579Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
+{"id":"franken_networkx-y87ra","title":"immediate_dominators includes start node; upstream nx excludes it","description":"Minor parity divergence turned up by the /testing-conformance-harnesses
scan on DAG algorithms.

``fnx.immediate_dominators(G, start)`` includes a ``{start: start}``
entry in the returned dict. Upstream ``nx.immediate_dominators``
excludes the start node per its documented contract (see nx
docstring quoted below).

Reproducer::

    import franken_networkx as fnx, networkx as nx

    gn = nx.DiGraph(); gn.add_edges_from([(0,1),(0,2),(1,3),(2,3),(3,4),(3,5)])
    gf = fnx.DiGraph(); gf.add_edges_from([(0,1),(0,2),(1,3),(2,3),(3,4),(3,5)])

    # nx  -> {1: 0, 2: 0, 3: 0, 4: 3, 5: 3}        (no key 0)
    # fnx -> {0: 0, 1: 0, 2: 0, 3: 0, 4: 3, 5: 3}  (includes 0 -> 0)
    assert fnx.immediate_dominators(gf, 0) == nx.immediate_dominators(gn, 0)  # FAILS

Upstream nx's docstring (verbatim)::

    idom : dict keyed by nodes
        A dict containing the immediate dominators of each node reachable from
        `start`, **except for `start` itself**.

Impact:

- Parity tests that do ``dict(fnx.immediate_dominators(G, s)) ==
  dict(nx.immediate_dominators(G, s))`` fail on the start-key
  presence.
- Users iterating ``.items()`` get an unexpected ``(start, start)``
  pair that their nx code wouldn't have produced. Practical effect
  depends on downstream code; most uses are robust to the extra
  entry because ``idom[start] == start`` is a self-loop and
  dominator-tree construction typically filters self-dominators.

Note: the existing fnx test at
``tests/python/test_dag_additional.py::TestImmediateDominators::test_chain``
explicitly asserts ``idom["a"] == "a"`` (the start-included shape),
so that test encodes the divergence and would need an update in
lockstep with the fix.

Fix plan:

1. ``python/franken_networkx/__init__.py::immediate_dominators`` (or
   wherever it dispatches from _fnx) — drop the ``{start: start}``
   entry from the returned dict before returning.
2. Update ``test_dag_additional.py::test_chain``, ``test_diamond``,
   etc. to no longer assert on ``idom[root]``.
3. Regression test: differential against ``nx.immediate_dominators``
   on a small DAG + start pair.

Priority: P3 — semantic divergence but inclusion is a superset of
the nx output (no dominator value is wrong, just an extra self-loop
entry).

Surfaced by /testing-conformance-harnesses scan; one of ~35 probe
calls across the DAG family, no other DAG divergences (the
topological_sort / dag_longest_path mismatches are legitimate
tie-break divergences — both results are valid — not filed).","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-23T17:45:25.512740513Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-23T17:50:46.074097Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"closed_at":"2026-04-23T17:50:46.074097Z","close_reason":"Fixed in bce345a: Python wrapper pops {start: start} before returning, matching upstream nx contract. test_dag_additional tests updated in lockstep; new differential-vs-nx test added."}
